authors.notes: Genesis through Acts completed by Henry; Romans through Revelation completed posthumously by 13 nonconformist ministers (1714).

aliases: Robert Jamieson, Andrew R. Fausset, and David Brown
authors.notes: Corporate authorship of three individuals: Robert Jamieson (1802-1880), Andrew Robert Fausset (1821-1910), and David Brown (1803-1897). See also individual entries jamieson-robert, fausset-andrew-robert, brown-david-jfb.
One of three co-authors of the JFB Commentary. See jamieson-fausset-brown for the group entry.
One of three co-authors of the JFB Commentary. See jamieson-fausset-brown for the group entry.
One of three co-authors of the JFB Commentary. Disambiguated from other David Browns by year range in alias. See jamieson-fausset-brown for the group entry.

aliases: Carl Friedrich Keil and Franz Delitzsch
authors.notes: Co-authorship of Carl Friedrich Keil (1807-1888) and Franz Delitzsch (1813-1890). See also keil-carl-friedrich and delitzsch-franz.
One of two co-authors of the Keil-Delitzsch OT Commentary. See keil-delitzsch for the group entry.
One of two co-authors of the Keil-Delitzsch OT Commentary. See keil-delitzsch for the group entry.
Corporate body convened 1643-1652 by the English Parliament to restructure the Church of England. Produced the Westminster Standards.
Particular Baptist minister at Petty France, London. Commissioned by the 1693 General Assembly to compose the Baptist Catechism. Also co-authored the 1689 London Baptist Confession.
Published for the American Presbyterian Board of Publications in 1840.
Exact birth year uncertain; sources range c.1627-1630.
Principal author of the Heidelberg Catechism (1563), co-authored with Caspar Olevianus and others under commission of Elector Frederick III.
One of the founders of the Scottish Secession Church.
International Reformed synod convened 1618-1619 at Dordrecht to settle the Arminian controversy. Produced the Canons of Dort (TULIP framework).
Drafted at the request of founding President James P. Boyce for the Southern Baptist Theological Seminary (1858).
Fourth ecumenical council convened 451 AD. Defined the two natures of Christ (Chalcedonian Definition).
Coalition of evangelical scholars convened in Chicago, 1978. Produced the Chicago Statement on Biblical Inerrancy.
Also attributed (with Theodore Beza and Antoine de la Roche Chandieu) as primary author of the French Confession of Faith (1559), though the data file records that work with a null author.
Walloon pastor and student of Calvin. Executed by the Spanish Inquisition in 1567.
Presided over the Second Council of Orange (529) which decisively rejected Semi-Pelagianism.
Led the Reformation in Basel. Close associate of Zwingli.
Successor to Zwingli as head of the Church of Zurich.
Approximate dates; bishop of Neocaesarea, student of Origen.
Composed the Helvetic Consensus (1675) with Lucas Gernler and Francis Turretin.
Approximate dates; bishop of Antioch, martyred under Trajan.
Approximate dates; bishop of Lyon, key defender of orthodoxy against Gnosticism.
Listed as a signatory-editor for the 1689 London Baptist Confession as reproduced in this dataset.
381 AD council that finalized the Nicene Creed. Also known as the Second Ecumenical Council.
Approximate dates; Carthaginian theologian, coined 'Trinity' (Trinitas). Later associated with Montanism.
Strasbourg reformer, mediator between Luther and Zwingli.
One of six ministers who drafted the Scots Confession (1560); others were John Knox, John Spottiswoode, John Willock, John Douglas, John Row.
Leader of the Reformation in Zurich. The data files use 'Ulrich Zwingli' as the author string.
Reformer of Bern. Ten Theses co-drafted with Franz Kolb; Zwingli suggested revisions. Data file uses 'Berthold Haller'.
Approximate dates. The data files use 'Paul' as the author string.
Traditional attribution. The data file uses 'Moses' as the author string.
Referenced in SCHEMA_SPEC.md as a future church-fathers dataset author. Not yet in a data file; included for forward compatibility.

Council held at Carthage in 411 AD; addressed the Donatist controversy.
Council held at Carthage in 419 AD; canon collection and disciplinary decisions.
Second Ecumenical Council, 381 AD; confirmed Nicene orthodoxy and condemned Arianism.
Third Ecumenical Council, 431 AD; condemned Nestorianism and affirmed the Theotokos.
Lateran Council of 649 AD; convened under Pope Martin I, condemned Monothelitism.
Fifth Ecumenical Council, 553 AD; condemned the Three Chapters and affirmed Chalcedonian christology.
Works falsely attributed to Ambrose; actual authorship unknown.
Works falsely attributed to Athanasius; actual authorship unknown.
Works falsely attributed to Augustine; actual authorship unknown.
Works falsely attributed to Barnabas; actual authorship unknown.
Works falsely attributed to Basil; actual authorship unknown.
Works falsely attributed to Chrysostom; actual authorship unknown.
Works falsely attributed to Clement; actual authorship unknown.
Works falsely attributed to Cyprian; actual authorship unknown.

Prominent Desert Father of Egypt, c. 4th-5th century; exact dates uncertain.
Bishop of Hierapolis in Phrygia, 2nd century; dates approximate.
Syriac church father, c. 6th century; exact dates unknown.
Bishop of Beroea (modern Aleppo); prominent figure in the Nestorian controversy. Dates approximate.
Bishop of Caesarea Maritima, Arian theologian; died 366.
Abbot of Iona; author of 'Life of Columba'. Same person as Adamnan of Iona. Dates approximate.
Abbot of Iona (679-704); author of 'Life of Columba' and 'De Locis Sanctis'. Dates approximate. Source data may show encoding variants of this name.

10th-century Melkite Arab Christian bishop of Manbij (Hierapolis) and historian; author of the universal history 'Kitab al-Unwan' (Book of the Title), written in Arabic. Died after 942. Source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Agapius_of_Hierapolis
Anglo-Saxon scholar and theologian at the court of Charlemagne.
Bishop of Alexandria who presided at the Council of Nicaea (325); mentor of Athanasius. Birth year unknown.
Bishop of Jerusalem, martyred c. 251. Birth year unknown.
Bishop of Milan; one of the four original Doctors of the Latin Church.
Anonymous attribution for hymns in the Ambrosian tradition not definitively assigned to Ambrose himself.
Anonymous 4th-century Latin commentator on Paul's epistles; name coined by Erasmus.
4th-century Egyptian bishop and ascetic; dates uncertain.
Desert Father and successor of Anthony the Great; c. 4th century. Dates unknown.
Alexandrian theologian; possible teacher of Origen. Dates approximate.
Bishop of Iconium, friend of the Cappadocian Fathers. Dates approximate.
Collective or unknown attribution for anonymous Greek patristic commentary fragments.

Bishop of Caesarea in Cappadocia; author of an important commentary on Revelation. Exact dates unknown; scholarly estimates range from the 5th to the 7th century; Wikipedia gives c.563-614 as one estimate. His commentary on Revelation became the standard Eastern text type.
Archbishop of Gortyna on Crete; hymnographer, author of the Great Canon. Dates approximate.
Archbishop of Canterbury; scholastic theologian, originator of the ontological argument for God's existence.
French theologian; founder of the school at Laon, influential in early scholasticism. Dates approximate.
Father of Christian monasticism. Dates approximate.
Syriac Christian writer; earliest Syriac church father. Dates approximate.
Bishop of Laodicea; condemned for Apollinarianism (denied full human soul in Christ). Dates approximate.
5th-century Latin author of a commentary on the Song of Songs. Exact dates unknown.
6th-century bishop of Beja (Portugal); author of a commentary on Revelation. Exact dates unknown.
Latin Christian poet; wrote 'De Actibus Apostolorum', a verse paraphrase of Acts. Dates approximate.
4th-century bishop of Carrhae (Harran); known for 'Acta Archelai', a debate against Manichaeism. Dates unknown.
Archbishop of Caesarea in Cappadocia; Byzantine scholar and commentator on Revelation. Dates approximate.
Alexandrian presbyter whose teaching on the subordination of the Son was condemned at Nicaea (325). Dates approximate.
North African apologist; author of 'Adversus Nationes'. Birth year unknown.
5th-century Latin writer; author of commentaries on the Psalms. Not to be confused with Arnobius of Sicca.
Arian theologian of Cappadocia; student of Lucian of Antioch. Birth year unknown.
Bishop of Alexandria; chief defender of Nicene orthodoxy against Arianism. Dates approximate.
2nd-century Christian philosopher and apologist. Dates approximate.
Bishop of Hippo; theologian and philosopher, one of the most influential figures in Western Christianity.
Latin Christian poet; author of 'Psychomachia' and the 'Cathemerinon'.
Archbishop of Caesarea; one of the three Cappadocian Fathers.
5th-century bishop of Seleucia in Isauria; preacher and theologian. Exact dates unknown.
Anglo-Saxon monk and scholar at Jarrow; author of 'Ecclesiastical History of the English People'.
Father of Western monasticism; author of the Rule of Saint Benedict. Dates approximate.
9th-century monk; author of a commentary on Revelation. Exact dates unknown.
Cistercian abbot; influential mystic, theologian, and church reformer.
Coptic monk and successor of Shenoute at the White Monastery; 5th century. Exact dates unknown.
Bishop of Zaragoza; disciple of Isidore of Seville. Dates approximate.
Roman presbyter of the late 2nd/early 3rd century; wrote against Montanism. Birth year unknown.
Bishop of Rome (pope), martyred c. 222. Birth year unknown.
Roman statesman and monk; founded the Vivarium monastery; preserved classical and Christian manuscripts.
Bishop of Aquileia; friend of Jerome and Rufinus. Birth year unknown.
Alexandrian theologian; head of the Catechetical School of Alexandria. Dates approximate.
Bishop of Rome; author of 1 Clement. Dates approximate.
3rd-century Latin Christian poet; author of 'Instructiones'. Exact dates unknown.
Greek hymnographer and bishop; foster-brother of John Damascene. Dates approximate.
Bishop of Carthage; martyred 258. Dates approximate.
Archbishop of Alexandria; presided over the Council of Ephesus (431).
Archbishop of Jerusalem; known for his Catechetical Lectures.
Collective tradition of early Christian monks and ascetics in Egypt and Syria, c. 3rd-5th centuries.
Frankish noblewoman and spiritual writer; author of 'Liber Manualis'. Dates approximate.
5th-century bishop of Photice in Epirus; mystic and spiritual writer. Exact dates unknown.
Head of the Catechetical School of Alexandria despite being blind from infancy.
Bishop of Tarsus; founder of the Antiochene school of exegesis. Birth year unknown.
Bishop of Alexandria; student of Origen. Dates approximate.
Bishop of Corinth; 2nd-century letter-writer. Exact dates unknown.
Palestinian monk; author of 'Discourses and Sayings'. Dates approximate.
Syriac deacon and hymnographer; Doctor of the Church. Dates approximate.
6th-century Latin translator; associate of Cassiodorus, translated Greek church history texts into Latin.
Bishop of Salamis (Cyprus); author of 'Panarion', a catalogue of heresies.
Bishop of Lyons; ascetic and biblical commentator. Birth year unknown.
Abbot near Naples; author of the 'Life of Severinus'. Dates approximate.
Bishop of Caesarea Maritima; 'Father of Church History', author of 'Historia Ecclesiastica'. Dates approximate.
Bishop of Emesa (Homs); exegete of the Antiochene school. Dates approximate.
Collective attribution for a corpus of Gallic sermons (5th-6th century); not a single author.
Bishop of Vercelli; defender of Nicene orthodoxy. Dates approximate.
Bishop of Antioch; deposed by Arian council c. 330. Birth year unknown.
Monk and theologian; influential in Eastern mysticism and asceticism. Later views condemned.
5th-century Armenian bishop; author of 'Refutation of the Sects'. Exact dates unknown.
Bishop of Rome (pope); martyred under Emperor Decius. Birth year unknown.
6th-century North African bishop; wrote in defense of the Three Chapters. Exact dates unknown.
5th-century British bishop; author of 'De Vita Christiana'. Exact dates unknown.
Patristic figure associated with Lyon; exact identity and dates uncertain.
Bishop of Riez in Provence; Semi-Pelagian theologian. Dates approximate.
Archbishop of Braga; founder of monasteries in northwest Iberia. Dates approximate.
Bishop of Ruspe in North Africa; Augustinian theologian.
Roman rhetorician and theologian; converted to Christianity c. 355. Dates approximate.
Bishop of Brescia; friend of Ambrose of Milan. Birth year unknown.
Patristic figure associated with Rimini; exact identity and dates uncertain.
Patriarch of Constantinople; opponent of Alexandrian theological influence. Birth year unknown.
5th-century presbyter of Marseilles; author of 'De Viris Illustribus'. Birth year unknown.
Archbishop of Thessalonica; theologian of hesychasm and defender of the Palamite distinction between essence and energies.
Bishop of Elvira (Granada); last Western adherent of Nicene extremism against Arianism. Dates approximate.
Archbishop of Constantinople; one of the three Cappadocian Fathers, called 'The Theologian'.
Bishop of Nyssa; one of the three Cappadocian Fathers, brother of Basil the Great. Dates approximate.
Pope Gregory I; Doctor of the Church, known as 'the Great'. Author of 'Pastoral Rule' and 'Dialogues'. Dates approximate.
Benedictine monk at Auxerre; biblical commentator. Dates approximate.
Bishop of Halberstadt; biblical commentator, sometimes confused with Haimo of Auxerre. Birth year unknown.
4th-century author of 'Acta Archelai', a polemical account of debates with Mani. Exact dates unknown.
2nd-century Palestinian Jewish-Christian historian; wrote 'Memoirs' defending apostolic tradition. Dates approximate.
Gnostic Valentinian teacher; wrote earliest known commentary on the Gospel of John. Included as historical source. Dates approximate.
Priest and biblical commentator in Jerusalem. Birth year unknown.
Archbishop of Arles; disciple of Honoratus of Lerins.
Bishop of Poitiers; Doctor of the Church, 'the Athanasius of the West'. Dates approximate.
Early Roman theologian and anti-pope; author of 'Refutation of All Heresies'. Dates approximate.
Coptic monk and successor of Pachomius the Great; author of 'Liber Orsiesii'. Dates approximate.
Dominican cardinal; produced one of the first biblical concordances. Dates approximate.
Archbishop of Toledo; defender of Marian devotion in Visigothic Spain.
Syriac mystic and bishop; one of the most widely read ascetic writers in the Eastern tradition. Dates uncertain.
Desert Father; c. 4th-5th century. Exact dates unknown.
9th-century bishop of Hedatta; Syriac biblical commentator of the Church of the East. Exact dates unknown.
Egyptian monk and theologian; wrote thousands of letters. Dates approximate.
Archbishop of Seville; Doctor of the Church; compiler of the encyclopedic 'Etymologiae'. Dates approximate.
Syriac Orthodox bishop and prolific commentator; wrote on all books of the Bible. Birth year unknown.
Syriac Orthodox bishop of Edessa; scholar, translator, and canonist.
Bishop of Serugh (Syria); prolific Syriac hymnographer.
Priest and Doctor of the Church; produced the Latin Vulgate Bible.
Monk and theologian; founder of St Victor Abbey in Marseilles; author of 'Institutes' and 'Conferences'. Dates approximate.
Syrian monk and priest; last of the Church Fathers; author of 'Fountain of Knowledge'. Dates approximate.
Patriarch of Antioch; initially allied with Nestorius but later accepted the Council of Ephesus. Birth year unknown.
Patristic or medieval figure; exact identity and dates uncertain.
Syriac mystic of the Church of the East. Dates approximate.
7th-century monk of the island of Karpathos; author of ascetic texts. Exact dates unknown.
5th-century Syriac mystic; possibly bishop of Apamea. Exact dates unknown.
Jewish historian; author of 'Jewish Antiquities' and 'Jewish War'. Frequently cited by the Church Fathers. Jewish writer, not a Christian author.
Bishop of Eclanum; Pelagian theologian and opponent of Augustine. Dates approximate.
Archbishop of Toledo; theologian and champion of Visigothic church autonomy.
North African priest who settled in Gaul; c. 5th century; teacher of Caesarius of Arles. Exact dates unknown.
Christian historian and chronographer; produced one of the first universal Christian chronologies. Dates approximate.
4th-century Latin writer; converted to Christianity; author of 'De Errore Profanarum Religionum'. Exact dates unknown.
Early Christian apologist; martyred in Rome c. 165. Dates approximate.
Italian-born Archbishop of Canterbury; scholastic theologian and church reformer. Dates approximate.
Archbishop of Seville; elder brother of Isidore of Seville; converted Visigothic Spain to Catholicism. Dates approximate.
Pope Leo I; Doctor of the Church; halted Attila's invasion; shaped Council of Chalcedon (451). Dates approximate.
Bishop of Cagliari; radical defender of Nicene orthodoxy; later schismatic. Birth year unknown.
Christian apologist; called the 'Christian Cicero'. Dates approximate.
Desert Father of Egypt; disciple of Anthony the Great. Dates approximate.
Sister of Basil the Great and Gregory of Nyssa; influential ascetic and theologian. Dates approximate.
Bishop of Pavia; Latin poet, rhetorician, and church writer.
3rd-century presbyter and rhetorician in Antioch; opponent of Paul of Samosata. Exact dates unknown.
5th-century monk and ascetic writer. Exact dates unknown.
3rd-century Latin Christian apologist; author of the 'Octavius'. Exact dates unknown.
Archbishop of Braga; apostle to the Suevi of Galicia. Dates approximate.
Bishop of Turin; preacher and liturgical author. Birth year unknown.
Byzantine monk and theologian; key defender of Chalcedonian orthodoxy against Monothelitism.
Bishop of Sardis; early Christian apologist and homiletic writer. Birth year unknown.
Bishop and martyr; wrote 'Symposium', an imitation of Plato in praise of virginity. Birth year unknown.
4th-century bishop of Emesa; wrote 'On the Nature of Man'. Exact dates unknown.
Armenian Archbishop of Tarsus; theologian and ecumenist.
Bishop of Remesiana (Serbia); possibly author of the 'Te Deum'. Dates approximate.
Dominican biblical commentator. Dates approximate.
Franciscan friar; prolific biblical commentator; influenced Luther's exegesis.
Monk at Mount Sinai; ascetic writer and correspondent of John Chrysostom. Birth year unknown.
Roman presbyter and antipope; rigorist who refused reconciliation of lapsed Christians. Dates approximate.
6th-century Greek commentator on the Book of Revelation and the epistles. Exact dates unknown.
6th-century Alexandrian deacon and biblical commentator. Exact dates unknown.
4th-century North African bishop; wrote against the Donatists. Exact dates unknown.
4th-century Coptic monk and abbot of the Pachomian community. Exact dates uncertain; name may reflect encoding or transliteration variant.
Alexandrian theologian and prolific biblical commentator; some teachings later condemned. Dates approximate.
Founder of cenobitic (communal) monasticism in Egypt.
Bishop of Barcelona; wrote against heresies and on penance. Dates approximate.
4th-century bishop; Arian sympathizer, present at the Council of Aquileia (381). Exact dates unknown.
Bishop of Helenopolis; author of the 'Lausiac History', a record of Desert Father lives. Dates approximate.
Theologian and martyr; devoted defender of Origen; mentor of Eusebius of Caesarea.
Bishop of Hierapolis; early collector of dominical sayings. Dates approximate.
11th-century Byzantine lexicographer. Not to be confused with Papias of Hierapolis.
Benedictine abbot of Corbie; wrote the first systematic treatise on the Eucharist.
Abbot of Dumium near Braga; translated Greek apophthegmata into Latin. Dates approximate.
Notary of Pope Gregory I; compiled excerpts of Gregory's biblical commentary. Birth year unknown.
Patron saint of Ireland; apostle to the Irish. Dates approximate.
Secretary to Ambrose of Milan; author of the 'Life of Ambrose'. Birth year unknown.
Bishop of Nola; poet and ascetic; friend of Augustine and Jerome.
Spanish priest and historian; disciple of Augustine; author of 'Historiarum Adversum Paganos'. Dates approximate.
Theologian condemned for denying original sin and affirming unaided human free will (Pelagianism). Dates approximate.
Archbishop of Ravenna; Doctor of the Church; known for short, eloquent sermons. Dates approximate.
Franciscan theologian; controversial figure in debates over Franciscan poverty.
Bishop of Alexandria; martyred under Diocletian. Birth year unknown.
Spanish Jewish convert to Christianity; author of 'Disciplina Clericalis'. Dates approximate.
Bishop of Brescia; author of a catalogue of heresies. Birth year unknown.
Bishop of Thmuis (Egypt); martyr under Diocletian; wrote a letter on the persecution. Birth year unknown.
Alexandrian Jewish philosopher; frequently cited by the Church Fathers. Jewish writer, not a Christian author. Dates approximate.
Syriac Orthodox bishop; theologian and mystic; commissioned the Philoxenian version of the NT. Dates approximate.
Patriarch of Constantinople; scholar and theologian; central figure in the Photian Schism. Dates approximate.
Bishop of Smyrna; disciple of the Apostle John; martyred. Dates approximate.
2nd-century bishop of Ephesus; wrote to Pope Victor I defending the Quartodeciman practice. Exact dates unknown.
Bishop of Rome (pope) for only about 43 days (235-236). Birth year unknown.
Bishop of Rome (pope) who wrote against the theology of Dionysius of Alexandria. Birth year unknown.
Bishop of Rome (pope); martyred in the Sardinian mines under Emperor Maximinus Thrax. Birth year unknown.
Bishop of Rome (pope) c. 222-230. Birth year unknown.
Bishop of Rome (pope) c. 199-217. Birth year unknown. See also separate entry 'zephyrinus'.
Bishop of Calama; biographer of Augustine of Hippo. Dates approximate.
Bishop of Lisbon; initially Arian but later reconciled to Nicene orthodoxy. Birth year unknown.
6th-century bishop of Hadrumetum (Tunisia); commentator on Revelation. Exact dates unknown.
Patriarch of Constantinople; disciple of John Chrysostom; known for Marian preaching. Birth year unknown.
Rhetorician of the school of Gaza; author of catenae (biblical commentaries assembled from earlier writers).
Gallic theologian; secretary to Pope Leo I; defender of Augustinian predestination. Dates approximate.
Latin Christian poet; author of 'Psychomachia' and the 'Peristephanon'. Same person as Aurelius Prudentius Clemens.
Bishop of Carthage; disciple of Augustine; exiled by the Vandals. Birth year unknown.
Archbishop of Mainz; prolific Carolingian theologian and encyclopedist; 'Teacher of Germany'.
Archbishop of Rheims; baptized the Frankish king Clovis I. Dates approximate.
Scottish theologian and mystic at the Abbey of Saint-Victor in Paris. Birth year unknown.
11th-century Benedictine monk; commentator on the Song of Songs. Exact dates unknown.
Byzantine hymnographer; composed kontakia (liturgical hymns). Dates approximate.
7th-century Syriac monk and bishop; spiritual writer of the Church of the East. Exact dates unknown.
Gallic presbyter; wrote 'De Gubernatione Dei' on divine providence. Dates approximate.
Bishop of Gabala (Syria); prolific preacher in Constantinople, contemporary of John Chrysostom. Birth year unknown.
Patriarch of Antioch; leading theologian of Miaphysite (Oriental Orthodox) Christianity.
Coptic abbot of the White Monastery; most prolific writer in the Coptic language. Dates approximate.
5th-century church historian; continued Eusebius's 'Ecclesiastical History'. Dates approximate.
Patriarch of Jerusalem; defender of Chalcedonian orthodoxy against Monothelitism.
Gallic writer; author of the 'Life of Martin of Tours'. Dates approximate.
Byzantine monk and mystic; one of three saints of the Orthodox Church given the title 'Theologian'.
Egyptian Desert Mother; c. 4th century. Exact dates unknown.
Syriac Christian apologist; disciple of Justin Martyr; compiled the Diatessaron. Dates approximate.
Christian martyr and military saint; died c. 319 under Emperor Licinius. Birth year unknown.
Bishop of Mopsuestia; leading exegete of the Antiochene school; posthumously condemned at 2nd Council of Constantinople (553).
Bishop of Cyrrhus; prolific exegete and church historian of the Antiochene school.
4th-century Coptic monk; disciple and successor of Pachomius the Great. Exact dates unknown.
Bishop of Ancyra; opponent of Nestorius at the Council of Ephesus (431). Birth year unknown.
3rd-century head of the Catechetical School of Alexandria. Exact dates unknown.
Bishop of Alexandria; predecessor of Peter of Alexandria. Birth year unknown.
14th-century Byzantine bishop of Nicaea; theologian and Palamite. Exact dates unknown.
Archbishop of Alexandria; opponent of John Chrysostom; uncle of Cyril of Alexandria. Birth year unknown.
2nd-century bishop of Antioch; apologist; first to use the word 'Trinity' (Trias). Exact dates unknown.
Archbishop of Ohrid; Byzantine exegete; wrote commentaries on the NT and on several OT books. Exact death date unknown; Wikipedia says 'after 1107', Britannica gives c.1109; no source supports 1126.
10th-century abbot of Einsiedeln; commentator on the Epistles. Exact dates unknown.
Dominican friar and Doctor of the Church; author of the 'Summa Theologiae'.
North African Donatist biblical exegete; his rules of interpretation influenced Augustine. Birth year unknown.
4th-century bishop of Bostra (Arabia); wrote against Manichaeism. Exact dates unknown.
Monk and translator; translated Greek theological works into Latin; friend turned opponent of Jerome.
Gnostic theologian; founder of Valentinianism. Included as a historical figure referenced by the Fathers. Dates approximate.
Bishop of Cimiez (near Nice); preacher, disciple of Lerins asceticism. Birth year unknown.

authors.display_name: Venerable Barsanuphius and John the Prophet
authors.notes: Two 6th-century Palestinian monks of the Gaza region; known for their spiritual letters. Exact dates unknown.
African bishop; wrote a commentary on OT canticles. Birth year unknown.
5th-century North African bishop; wrote 'Historia Persecutionis Africanae Provinciae' on the Vandal persecution. Exact dates unknown.
5th-century bishop of Cartenna in Mauretania; wrote against the Arians. Exact dates unknown.
Bishop of Pettau (Slovenia); earliest Latin biblical commentator; martyred under Diocletian. Birth year unknown.
5th-century North African bishop; wrote against various heresies. Exact dates unknown.
Monk of Lerins; author of the 'Commonitorium'; known for the Vincentian Canon on tradition. Birth year unknown.

Benedictine monk and abbot of Reichenau; poet and theologian. The Glossa Ordinaria is now attributed to the school of Anselm of Laon (12th century), not to Strabo; the misattribution goes back to Trithemius (c.1494). Some of Strabo's Carolingian glosses were absorbed into the later Glossa. Source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Walafrid_Strabo
Bishop of Rome (pope) c. 199-217. Same person as Pope Zephyrinus; separate entry for upstream data variant. Birth year unknown.
4th-century author of 'Dialogue on the Right Faith Against Mani'; exact identity uncertain.
